name: Tournament Poker
description: No-limit hold'em tournaments are the canonical incomplete-information game, where irrevocable all-in decisions are made on two private cards and probabilistic inference. A Levitt/Miles study found skilled players achieved +30% ROI at the WSOP vs. -15% for the field, yet skill explains only ~17% of hand-level variance, meaning a world champion can lose to an amateur on any given day.

thumbnail.alt: Poker chips and hole cards on a green felt tournament table

media.title: 2023 WSOP Main Event - Daniel Weinman Wins $12.1 Million

sources.title: The Role of Skill Versus Luck in Poker: Evidence from the World Series of Poker
sources.author: Steven D. Levitt, Thomas J. Miles

sources.title: Is poker a game of skill or chance? A quasi-experimental study
The dominance of skill in online poker

time_to_competence.notes: Basic rules and hand rankings learnable in an hour. Playing a coherent tournament strategy (position, pot odds, stack-depth awareness) takes weeks to months.

time_to_expert.notes: Estimates range from 1-7 years to become a consistent winning professional. Multiple sources cite a minimum of 1 million hands of deliberate practice. The field has become dramatically more skilled since the 2003 poker boom.

feedback_delay.notes: Individual hand results are immediate but deeply misleading; correct decisions lose money due to variance. Reliable assessment of skill requires 500+ hours of play to separate signal from noise.

failure_cost.notes: Tournament buy-ins range from $1 to $250,000. Professional players routinely experience months-long losing streaks that can deplete bankrolls. Financial ruin is a real risk without bankroll management.

cognitive_demand.notes: Simultaneous computation of pot odds, implied odds, opponent range estimation, ICM (tournament equity), and GTO (game-theory-optimal) frequencies. Modern professionals use solver software to study optimal frequencies.

physical_demand.notes: Seated activity. Major tournaments involve 12-14 hour sessions over multiple days, requiring stamina but no athletic ability.

prerequisite_knowledge.notes: Basic probability and combinatorics help but aren't formally required. Game-theory-optimal concepts have become essential at higher levels but are learned within the domain.

measurability.notes: No universally accepted rating system. ROI and hourly win rate are standard metrics but require enormous sample sizes due to variance. The Hendon Mob and GPI rank tournament results.

perishability.notes: Core strategic thinking is durable, but the optimal strategy evolves as the player pool improves. Exploitative adjustments to current population tendencies require ongoing study.

precision_required.notes: Bet sizing must be precise to manipulate pot odds. GTO strategies require specific frequencies (e.g., bluffing exactly 33% on river bets). Small deviations are exploitable by skilled opponents.

ambiguity.notes: The correct play can lose and the incorrect play can win. Outcomes are noisy. Even experts disagree on optimal lines in complex spots. Solver solutions often produce mixed strategies.

instruction_dependency.notes: Self-study is highly viable through solver software (PioSolver, GTO+), training sites, and hand history review. Coaching accelerates but is not required.

environmental_variability.notes: Card distribution is random. Table composition changes constantly in tournaments. Stack sizes, blind levels, bubble dynamics, and opponent tendencies create unique situations every hand.

concurrent_skills.notes: Simultaneously managing mathematical computation, opponent reads, emotional regulation, table image management, bankroll awareness, and ICM considerations in tournament play.

plateau_density.notes: Variance obscures skill improvements for extended periods. Players can improve significantly without seeing results for months, making it difficult to confirm progress.

domain_evolution.notes: The game has evolved dramatically since the early 2000s. GTO solvers have transformed strategy. Strategies from even 5 years ago are exploitable today. Continuous adaptation is required.

observability.notes: Televised poker with hole-card cameras makes skill visible. Live play is harder to assess; a great fold looks the same as a bad one to spectators.

luck_variance.notes: Levitt/Miles found skill is real but variance is enormous. A world-class player entering a single tournament has roughly a 15-20% chance of cashing. Short-term results are dominated by card distribution.

social_coordination.notes: Fundamentally an individual game played against other individuals. Table dynamics involve social reading and manipulation. Study groups and coaching stables exist but competition is solo.

methodology_notes: Scores informed by the Levitt/Miles 2014 WSOP study (Journal of Sports Economics), the 2012 PubMed quasi-experimental study on poker skill vs. chance, and the ScienceDirect study on skill dominance in online poker. Training timelines from established poker coaching resources (BlackRain79, Upswing Poker). The high luck_variance score reflects the well-documented tension between poker's long-term skill component and short-term randomness.
